subject: Surgery
title: Abdominal hernias
preambleMarkdown:
# Abdominal hernias

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Abdominal hernias involves protrusion of tissue through a weakness or defect in a containing wall or anatomic opening.
- Abdominal hernias is a clinical condition or syndrome in surgery.
- This article focuses on indications, anatomy, perioperative risk, operative options, postoperative care, and complications.
- The practical clinical priority is surgery diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Abdominal hernias to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Abdominal hernias is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is surgery diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Abdominal hernias depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In surgery, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the hernia neck, sac contents, fascial defect, nearby vessels, nerves, bowel, and prior repair or mesh.
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Abdominal hernias.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Abdominal hernias disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Abdominal hernias should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Abdominal hernias first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Abdominal hernias, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Typical findings are a bulge, pressure, discomfort, cough impulse, reducibility, or symptoms worsened by straining.
- Irreducible painful hernia, vomiting, obstruction, skin changes, fever, or peritonitis suggests incarceration, strangulation, or ischemia.
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Abdominal hernias, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused surgery examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Abdominal hernias presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Abdominal hernias with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Many hernias are clinical diagnoses; ultrasound or CT is useful when anatomy is unclear, obesity limits examination, recurrence is suspected, or obstruction/strangulation is possible.
- First decide whether Abdominal hernias is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Abdominal hernias should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic low-risk hernias may be observed in selected patients.
- Symptomatic, enlarging, incarcerated, strangulated, obstructed, or high-risk hernias need surgical evaluation.
- Strangulation or bowel obstruction requires urgent operative management.
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Abdominal hernias is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses surgery diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Abdominal hernias, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications include incarceration, strangulation, bowel obstruction, ischemia, perforation, recurrence, chronic pain, wound infection, and mesh complications.
- Complications of Abdominal hernias may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Abdominal hernias patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Abdominal hernias, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Abdominal hernias in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Abdominal hernias, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Abdominal hernias does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Abdominal hernias may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Abdominal hernias, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Abdominal hernias as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Painful irreducible hernia is a surgical red flag.
- Strangulated hernia threatens bowel viability.
- Femoral hernias have a higher strangulation risk than inguinal hernias.
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Abdominal hernias draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Hernia

_Source lines: 10766-10902._

```text
IX. HERNIA
A. Discussion
1. Definition. A hernia is the protrusion of a viscus through an opening into an
abnormal location. Hernias are present in approximately 5% of the population.

Approximately 75% are inguinal, 10% are incisional, 5% are umbilical, and 5%
are femoral. Hiatal hernia is present in at least one-third of the population and
refers to the herniation of the proximal stomach above the diaphragm.

a. Inguinal hernias
i. Indirect inguinal hernias are the most common type of hernia in both
men and women. They occur when the bowel travels down a patent
Quick 	HIT
McBurney point is located
two-thirds of the distance
along the line drawn from
the umbilicus to the anterior-
superior iliac spine.

Quick 	HIT
Normal WBC Count does not
rule out appendicitis.

132 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Gastrointestinal Emergencies
processus vaginalis into the inguinal canal. The bowel may also progress
into the scrotum.

ii. Direct inguinal hernias. The bowel protrudes through a defect in the
abdominal wall in Hesselbach triangle. The incidence increases with
advancing age and physical exertion.

b. Incisional hernias occur at sites of previous abdominal surgery.

c. Umbilical hernias are 10 times more common in women than in men, and
they are often associated with pregnancy or with ascites.

d. Femoral hernias. The bowel protrudes into the femoral canal.

## 2. Complications

a. Incarceration. An incarcerated hernia is one in which the protruding ab-
dominal contents cannot be returned to the abdominal cavity. Left untreated,
an incarcerated hernia can progress and become strangulated. Thirty to forty
percent of femoral hernias become incarcerated or strangulated.

b. Strangulation. A strangulated hernia is one in which the tissue becomes
necrotic due to obstruction of its blood supply.

B. Clinical features
1. Uncomplicated hernias
a. Symptoms. The patient may complain of a bulge or mass that grows and shrinks
over time. The hernia may spontaneously reduce or be reducible by the patient.

b. Physical examination findings. The patient should always be examined in
both recumbent and standing positions.

i. Palpation. The hernia may not be visible or palpable without
provocative maneuvers (e.g., coughing or a Valsalva maneuver).

a) Femoral hernias may be palpable below the inguinal ligament.

b) Inguinal hernias
1) Direct inguinal hernias are palpable in Hesselbach triangle su-
perior to the inguinal ligament. In men, the examining finger
should be placed in the inguinal canal by invaginating the scro-
tum, allowing palpation of the direct hernia at the floor of the
inguinal canal.

2) Indirect inguinal hernias can be palpated by moving the examin-
ing finger down the inguinal canal.

ii. Auscultation. Bowel sounds may be heard in the area of the mass.

2. Incarceration. An incarcerated hernia cannot be reduced with gentle pressure
by either the patient or physician. Otherwise, the findings are the same as for
uncomplicated hernias.

## 3. Strangulation

a. Symptoms. The patient may demonstrate symptoms of bowel obstruction
(i.e., vomiting, obstipation, abdominal tenderness).

b. Physical examination findings. The hernia will usually be tender to palpa-
tion. Fever is a consistent finding in patients with necrosis of the bowel.

C. Differential diagnoses include hydrocele, lipoma, lymphadenopathy, abscess, and
tumor.

D. Evaluation. In patients who have a tender, irreducible mass or symptoms of stran-
gulation, additional studies, including abdominal films, a WBC count, and amy-
lase levels, should be performed.

E. Therapy
1. Relief of obstruction. Patients with symptoms of strangulation or small bowel
obstruction should be treated for bowel obstruction (see VI.B.5).

2. Reduction is achieved through the application of continuous gentle pressure,
often in conjunction with postural positioning. Pain or anxiolytic medications
are often administered.

3. Surgery. Patients with irreducible hernias require surgical consultation.

F. 	Disposition. Patients with reducible hernias may be discharged with instructions to
avoid strenuous activities and to return to the ED if any symptoms of incarceration
or strangulation develop. Surgical follow-up is mandatory. Patients with irreducible
hernias or ascites associated with painful umbilical hernia require admission.

Quick 	HIT
Over 80% of femoral hernias
occur in women, and they
are thought to be related
to pregnancy and physical
exertion.

Quick 	HIT
Indirect inguinal hernias
are more likely than direct
inguinal hernias to become
strangulated.

Quick 	HIT
The abdomen is no place
for dead bowel. If necrosis
or signs of acute stran-
gulation are present, it is
inappropriate to attempt to
reduce the hernia.

G A S T R O I N T E S T I N A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	133

Gastrointestinal Emergencies
```

### Source Excerpt: Abdominal Wall Hernias

_Source lines: 3345-3610._

```text
I. Abdominal Wall Hernias
A. Anterior abdominal wall anatomy
l . Anterior abdominal wall
a. Skeletal support includes the lowest ribs, pelvic brim, and lumbar spine.

b. Compresses and contains abdominal viscera and contributes to support
movement of spine and pelvis
c. Lamination of the muscles and aponeurosis precludes evisceration; hernias
most commonly form between laminations (where only peritoneum and
fascia exist) .

2. Fascial layers
a. Superficial subcutaneous layer: Camper fascia
( l ) It can contain a significant amount of fat (panniculus adiposis) .

(2) Blood vessels include superficial inferior epigastric and superficial
circumflex iliac artery and vein (both arise from femoral vessels) .

(3) Lymphatic drainage to inguinal lymph nodes inferior to inguinal ligament
( 4) This superficial fascial layer fuses with fascia innominata, which invests
the external abdominal oblique, binds to inguinal ligament, and contin
ues onto the fascia lata. It includes Hesselbach triangle superiorly and
is the weakest part of the groin.

(5) Not a strength layer. The high adipose content of this layer and the lack
of strong Hbrinous material usually prevent suture material from holding.

b. Deep subcutaneous layer: Scarpa fascia
( l ) Compressed fibrous components of superHcial fascia where it forms the
fundiform ligament of the penis and the superficial perineal fascia
(2) Has more tensile strength than Camper fascia and more easily identifiable
c. Rectus sheath: The rectus abdominis muscle is enveloped in a sheath. This
sheath is formed from the aponeuroses of the abdominal wall musculature.

The aponeurotic fascial layers either completely encircle the rectus sheath
or run only on its anterior boarder, depending on its location to the arcuate
line. Above the arcuate line, the fascial layers split. Below the arcuate line,
the layers all run anterior to the muscle.

( l ) Formed by aponeuroses of external oblique, internal oblique, and
transversus abdominis with midline decussation as the linea alba
(2) Anterior sheath
(a) Superior to arcuate line: external oblique and anterior division of
the internal oblique.

(b) Inferior to arcuate line: composite of all layers
(3) Posterior sheath
(a) Superior to arcuate line: posterior division of the internal oblique,
the transversus abdominis aponeurosis , and the transversalis fascia
(b) Inferior to arcuate line: transversalis fascia
(4) Arcuate line (of Douglas) is transfer of connective tissue away from the
posterior sheath.

d. Semilunar line
( l ) Lateral to rectus abdominis
(2) Site of insertion of lateral abdominal muscle aponeurotic insertions
3. Blood supply
a. Rectus abdominis
( l ) Superior epigastric (continuation of internal mammary artery)
(2) Inferior epigastric (off external iliac artery)
(3) Contribution from lower intercostal arteries near umbilicus
b. Lateral muscles

( l ) Primarily from 8th to 1 2 th intercostal arteries, deep circumflex iliac ar
teries , and lumbar arteries

a. Dermatome pattern with overlapping Helds, so disruption is not normally
noticed

d. The external oblique receives intercostal branches.

l. All ages , 1 0% incidence in premature infants, and varies by gender
a. Inguinal hernias have 7 : 1 male-to-female ratio .

b. Femoral hernias have 1 . 8 : 1 female predominance. Femoral hernias are more
common in women than in men; however, inguinal hernias are still the most
common hernias women experience.

2. Types of hernias
a. Inguinal hernias: 80%
b. Femoral hernias: 5%
c. Incisional, umbilical, and epigastric hernias: 1 5 %

## 3 . Presentation

a. Duration of symptoms vary (chronic versus acute) .

b. History of lump or swelling that occurs on straining

## 4. Complications

a. Incarceration versus strangulation: Incarcerated hernias are not reducible.

They are j ailed or "incarcerated. " Strangulated hernias are incarcerated her
nias that are now being strangled; that is, deprived of blood flow. Strangu
lated hernias are a surgical emergency.

( 1 ) Incarceration ("trapped," "j ailed," or irreducible hernia)
(a) Hernia sac contents may vary (omentum, ovary, nonobstructed bowel) .

(b) Irreducible due to chronic adhesions
(c) Must differentiate between a hydrocele and a hernia (because you
will not be able to reduce a hydrocele into the peritoneal cavity)
i. One can get one's examining fingers above a hydrocele but not
a hernia .

ii. A hydrocele transilluminates , whereas a hernia does not.

(d) Treatment of incarcerated hernia is surgicaL
(2) Strangulation
(a) Contents (omentum, bowel, ovary, etc.) of hernia sac are ischemic
and/or necrotic.

i. Pressure on the bowel trapped in the hernia sac produces ve
nous congestion, which leads to edema of the bowel, and sub
sequent pressures so high that arterial inflow is obstructed and
the bowel becomes gangrenous.

(b) Presentation/physical exam
i. Strangulated hernias present with tender, irreducible masses and
have systemic symptoms such as toxicity, dehydration, and fever.

ii. The hernia is tense and very tender and may have overlying
skin changes (erythematous or bluish discoloration) . It may be
warm and with induration.

iii. Bowel sounds are absent in the hernia.

iv. Leukocytosis is common, and metabolic acidosis may be present.

v. Lactic acid may be normal because there is no venous outflow
to circulate the lactic acid.

vi. Symptoms of bowel obstruction may be the only sign: obstipa
tion, distension, nausea/vomiting
(c) Treatment
i. Strangulated hernias should not be reduced.

ii. Nasogastric (NG) tube suction, fluid and electrolyte replace
ment, and antibiotics are started.

iii. Emergency surgery follows with exposure of the hernia, open
ing of the sac, and resecting any gangrenous viscera, followed
by hernia repair.

iv. The hernia repair is no longer a sterile field in this setting.

Synthetic mesh must NOT be used.

(3) Intestinal obstruction
(a) Once the most common cause of obstruction, hernia is now the third
most common cause (Erst is adhesions, second is cancer) . ABCs of
bowel obstruction: A= adhesions, B= bulge (hernia) , C = cancer

(b) Presentation/physical exam
i. An obstructed hernia is tense and irreducible, and the abdo
men is distended.

ii. High-pitched bowel sounds are heard with frequent rushes.

(c) Plain abdominal X-ray
i. Shows classic dilated loops of bowel with air-fluid levels and
paucity of gas distal to obstruction
ii. Computed tomography may be helpful if clinical diagnosis is
uncertain.

(d) Treatment
i. Patient is sedated and placed in Trendelenburg position.

ii. Taxis maneuver elongates neck to allow contents to pass into
abdomen.

( l ) Grasping hernia neck with one hand and applying inter
mittent pressure with other hand on distal neck of hernia
iii. Abort the attempt at reduction if unsuccessful after two or
three tries because reduction of gangrenous bowel or reduc
tion en masse is possible.

iv. Postreduction
( l ) Reduction of hernia is followed by resuscitation and ur
gent surgery via a direct approach over the hernia site.

(2) Viable bowel is reduced back into the abdomen. Nonviable
bowel is resected with anastomosis prior to hernia repair.

5. Massive hernia
a. Large portion of abdominal contents situated in hernia sac; usually chronic
b. Hernia contents have lost their right of domain; thus, replacement of ab
dominal contents with fascial closure puts the patient at risk of abdominal
compartment syndrome, skin edema, and cellulitis after closure.

c. Pneumoperitoneum may help when returning contents to the abdominal
cavity over 3 weeks prior to repair.

d. A prosthesis is usually required because of the size of the defect.
```

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.
